Because they have the gift of the Holy Ghost, they can "speak with the tongue of angels" (2 Nephi 31:13); and angels, who are but true ministers on the other side of the veil, "speak by the power of the Holy Ghost; wherefore, they speak the word of Christ." (2 Nephi 32:3.) Jesus counsels his ministers: "Take no thought how or what ye shall speak: for it shall be given you in that same hour what ye shall speak. For it is not ye that…

How does the Aaronic Priesthood hold the key to the ministering of angels? The answer is the same as for the Spirit of the Lord. In general, the blessings of spiritual companionship and communication are available only to those who are clean. As explained earlier, through the Aaronic Priesthood ordinances of baptism and the sacrament, we are cleansed of our sins and promised that if we keep our covenants we will always have His Spirit to be with us. tasted [Gr. geuomai] to taste, i.e. perceive the flavor of, partake of, enjoy, to feel, make trial of, experience; to take food, eat, to take nourishment, eat. A major theme of ancient scripture is to sit down and partake of the heavenly feast. We are also told to "feast upon the words of Christ; for behold, the words of Christ will tell you all things what ye should do" (2 Nephi 32:3).
The "angels" are those who are on the Lords errand and speak the words of Christ. by the Holy Ghost which "will tell you all things what ye should do." i.e. living scriptures.

In Proverbs we are told to "ponder the path of life" (Prov. 5:6). As we ponder the path of life, we can set our path to righteousness and feel the Spirit direct us. "Feast upon the words of Christ; for behold, the words of Christ will tell you all things what ye should do" (2 Ne. 32:3). (Robert D. Hales, "Healing Soul and Body," Ensign, Nov. 1998, 15)
